title: The Functional Anatomy of Sparse Features in Language Models
description: We propose a systematic framework for mapping sparse autoencoder (SAE) features to specific model capabilities (factual recall, syntactic processing, reasoning, etc.), creating a 'Functional Feature Atlas' that reveals how capabilities are organized in feature space. We introduce the Functional Localization Index (FLI) to quantify whether capabilities are supported by few specialized features (localized) or many distributed features, and show that this localization spectrum predicts when SAE-based interpretability succeeds or fails.
motivation: Current SAE evaluation uses global metrics (reconstruction loss, sparsity, aggregate interpretability scores) that cannot distinguish whether specific model capabilities are well-captured by SAE features or lost in reconstruction residuals. Individual features are studied in isolation, missing the collective functional organization. This gap matters because localized capabilities are amenable to feature-level interpretation and intervention, while distributed capabilities require different analysis approaches. Understanding this organization is essential for knowing when to trust SAE-based interpretability and when alternative methods are needed.
proposed_approach: 1) Define a taxonomy of 6 model capabilities with targeted evaluation suites. 2) Compute gradient-weighted feature attribution for each capability across all layers. 3) Validate with targeted activation patching on top features. 4) Compute the Functional Localization Index (FLI) measuring capability concentration in feature space. 5) Build capability overlap matrices showing feature sharing structure. 6) Analyze SAE residuals per-capability to quantify dark matter dependence. 7) Show localization predicts interpretability quality and causal faithfulness. Uses GPT-2 Small with pre-trained SAEs from SAE Lens, feasible on a single A6000 in <8 hours.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models
related_work.relevance: Foundational SAE paper showing features are more interpretable than neurons. We build on their framework but study how features collectively organize into capabilities rather than evaluating features individually.

related_work.title: Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet
related_work.authors: Templeton, Conerly, Marcus, Lindsey, Bricken, et al.
related_work.relevance: Demonstrated SAEs scale to production models with millions of features. We complement their single-feature analysis with capability-level functional mapping.

related_work.title: SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders in Language Model Interpretability
related_work.relevance: Most comprehensive SAE evaluation to date with 8 metrics across 200+ SAEs. Key finding: proxy metrics don't predict downstream utility. We extend evaluation to per-capability faithfulness, explaining WHY global metrics fail.

related_work.title: Sparse Autoencoders Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis
related_work.relevance: Shows SAEs with different seeds learn different features. Our capability-level analysis provides a complementary perspective: even if individual features differ, the capability-level organization may be more stable.

related_work.title: A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.relevance: Identifies feature absorption as a systematic SAE failure mode for hierarchical concepts. Our analysis can detect whether absorption disproportionately affects specific capabilities.

related_work.title: Decomposing The Dark Matter of Sparse Autoencoders
related_work.relevance: Decomposes SAE residual into unlearned linear features, dense features, and nonlinear errors. We connect their dark matter analysis to specific capabilities, showing WHAT information is lost, not just HOW MUCH.

related_work.title: Open Problems in Mechanistic Interpretability
related_work.relevance: Identifies functional organization of features as an open problem. Our work directly addresses this by providing the first systematic capability mapping.

related_work.title: The Linear Representation Hypothesis and the Geometry of Large Language Models
related_work.relevance: Formalizes linear representation structure. Our localization analysis tests whether capabilities that align with linear feature directions are better captured by SAEs.

hypothesis: Model capabilities exist on a localization spectrum in SAE feature space: localized capabilities (supported by few specialized features) are more interpretable, more causally faithful, and less dependent on SAE dark matter, while distributed capabilities (requiring many features) are less interpretable and more dependent on information in SAE residuals. This predicts when SAE-based interpretability will succeed or fail.
success_criteria: FLI varies significantly across capabilities (at least 2x range between most and least localized)
Localization predicts causal faithfulness: ablating top features for localized capabilities causes >3x more performance degradation than for distributed capabilities
Localization predicts dark matter dependence: residual probes are >2x more accurate for distributed vs. localized capabilities
Patterns are consistent across at least 2 SAE architectures (ReLU L1, TopK)
Features supporting localized capabilities have higher automated interpretability scores

computational_requirements.key_resources: Pre-trained SAEs from SAE Lens library, LAMA/BLiMP/WiC/SST-2 evaluation datasets
computational_requirements.bottleneck: Activation patching for causal validation (~2 hours for 6 capabilities x 50 features x 200 examples)
